Math4302Exam reviewsMath 4302 Exam 2 Review

Math 4302 Exam 2 Review

Groups

Direct products

Zm×Zn\mathbb{Z}_m\times \mathbb{Z}_nZm​×Zn​ is cyclic if and only if mmm and nnn have greatest common divisor 111.

More generally, for Zn1×Zn2×⋯×Znk\mathbb{Z}_{n_1}\times \mathbb{Z}_{n_2}\times \cdots \times \mathbb{Z}_{n_k}Zn1​​×Zn2​​×⋯×Znk​​, if n1,n2,⋯ ,nkn_1,n_2,\cdots,n_kn1​,n2​,⋯,nk​ are pairwise coprime, then the direct product is cyclic.

If n=p1m1…pkmkn=p_1^{m_1}\ldots p_k^{m_k}n=p1m1​​…pkmk​​, where pip_ipi​ are distinct primes, then the group

G=Zn=Zp1m1×Zp2m2×⋯×ZpkmkG=\mathbb{Z}_n=\mathbb{Z}_{p_1^{m_1}}\times \mathbb{Z}_{p_2^{m_2}}\times \cdots \times \mathbb{Z}_{p_k^{m_k}}G=Zn​=Zp1m1​​​×Zp2m2​​​×⋯×Zpkmk​​​

is cyclic.

Structure of finitely generated abelian groups

Theorem for finitely generated abelian groups

Every finitely generated abelian group GGG is isomorphic to

Zp1n1×Zp2n2×⋯×Zpknk×Z×…×Z⏟m timesZ_{p_1}^{n_1}\times Z_{p_2}^{n_2}\times \cdots \times Z_{p_k}^{n_k}\times\underbrace{\mathbb{Z}\times \ldots \times \mathbb{Z}}_{m\text{ times}}Zp1​n1​​×Zp2​n2​​×⋯×Zpk​nk​​×m timesZ×…×Z​​

Corollary for divisor size of abelian subgroup

If ggg is abelian and ∣G∣=n|G|=n∣G∣=n, then for every divisor mmm of nnn, GGG has a subgroup of order mmm.

Warning

This is not true if GGG is not abelian.

Consider A4A_4A4​ (alternating group for S4S_4S4​) does not have a subgroup of order 6.

Cosets

Definition of Cosets

Let GGG be a group and HHH its subgroup.

Define a relation on GGG and a∼ba\sim ba∼b if a−1b∈Ha^{-1}b\in Ha−1b∈H.

This is an equivalence relation.

  • Reflexive: a∼aa\sim aa∼a: a−1a=e∈Ha^{-1}a=e\in Ha−1a=e∈H
  • Symmetric: a∼b⇒b∼aa\sim b\Rightarrow b\sim aa∼b⇒b∼a: a−1b∈Ha^{-1}b\in Ha−1b∈H, (a−1b)−1=b−1a∈H(a^{-1}b)^{-1}=b^{-1}a\in H(a−1b)−1=b−1a∈H
  • Transitive: a∼ba\sim ba∼b and b∼c⇒a∼cb\sim c\Rightarrow a\sim cb∼c⇒a∼c : a−1b∈H,b−1c∈Ha^{-1}b\in H, b^{-1}c\in Ha−1b∈H,b−1c∈H, therefore their product is also in HHH, (a−1b)(b−1c)=a−1c∈H(a^{-1}b)(b^{-1}c)=a^{-1}c\in H(a−1b)(b−1c)=a−1c∈H

So we get a partition of GGG to equivalence classes.

Let a∈Ga\in Ga∈G, the equivalence class containing aaa

aH={x∈G∣a∼x}={x∈G∣a−1x∈H}={x∣x=ah for some h∈H}aH=\{x\in G| a\sim x\}=\{x\in G| a^{-1}x\in H\}=\{x|x=ah\text{ for some }h\in H\}aH={x∈G∣a∼x}={x∈G∣a−1x∈H}={x∣x=ah for some h∈H}

This is called the coset of aaa in HHH.

Definition of Equivalence Class

Let a∈Ha\in Ha∈H, and the equivalence class containing aaa is defined as:

aH={x∣a≃x}={x∣a−1x∈H}={x∣x=ah for some h∈H}aH=\{x|a\simeq x\}=\{x|a^{-1}x\in H\}=\{x|x=ah\text{ for some }h\in H\}aH={x∣a≃x}={x∣a−1x∈H}={x∣x=ah for some h∈H}

Properties of Equivalence Class

aH=bHaH=bHaH=bH if and only if a∼ba\sim ba∼b.

Lemma for size of cosets

Any coset of HHH has the same cardinality as HHH.

Define ϕ:H→aH\phi:H\to aHϕ:H→aH by ϕ(h)=ah\phi(h)=ahϕ(h)=ah.

ϕ\phiϕ is an bijection, if ah=ah′  ⟹  h=h′ah=ah'\implies h=h'ah=ah′⟹h=h′, it is onto by definition of aHaHaH.

Corollary: Lagrange’s Theorem

If GGG is a finite group, and H≤GH\leq GH≤G, then ∣H∣∣∣G∣|H|\big\vert |G|∣H∣​∣G∣. (size of HHH divides size of GGG)

Normal Subgroups

Definition of Normal Subgroup

A subgroup H≤GH\leq GH≤G is called a normal subgroup if aH=HaaH=HaaH=Ha for all a∈Ga\in Ga∈G. We denote it by H⊴GH\trianglelefteq GH⊴G

Lemma for equivalent definition of normal subgroup

The following are equivalent:

  1. H⊴GH\trianglelefteq GH⊴G
  2. aHa−1=HaHa^{-1}=HaHa−1=H for all a∈Ga\in Ga∈G
  3. aHa−1⊆HaHa^{-1}\subseteq HaHa−1⊆H for all a∈Ga\in Ga∈G, that is aha−1∈Haha^{-1}\in Haha−1∈H for all a∈Ga\in Ga∈G

Factor group

Consider the operation on the set of left coset of GGG, denoted by SSS. Define

(aH)(bH)=abH(aH)(bH)=abH(aH)(bH)=abH

Condition for operation

The operation above is well defined if and only if H⊴GH\trianglelefteq GH⊴G.

Definition of factor (quotient) group

If H⊴GH\trianglelefteq GH⊴G, then the set of cosets with operation:

(aH)(bH)=abH(aH)(bH)=abH(aH)(bH)=abH

is a group denoted by G/HG/HG/H. This group is called the quotient group (or factor group) of GGG by HHH.

Fundamental homomorphism theorem (first isomorphism theorem)

If ϕ:G→G′\phi:G\to G'ϕ:G→G′ is a homomorphism, then the function f:G/ker⁡(ϕ)→ϕ(G)f:G/\ker(\phi)\to \phi(G)f:G/ker(ϕ)→ϕ(G), (ϕ(G)⊆G′\phi(G)\subseteq G'ϕ(G)⊆G′) given by f(aker⁡(ϕ))=ϕ(a)f(a\ker(\phi))=\phi(a)f(aker(ϕ))=ϕ(a), ∀a∈G\forall a\in G∀a∈G, is an well-defined isomorphism.

  • If GGG is abelian, N≤GN\leq GN≤G, then G/NG/NG/N is abelian.
  • If GGG is finitely generated and N⊴GN\trianglelefteq GN⊴G, then G/NG/NG/N is finitely generated.

Definition of simple group

GGG is simple if GGG has no proper (H≠G,{e}H\neq G,\{e\}H=G,{e}), normal subgroup.

Center of a group

Recall from previous lecture, the center of a group GGG is the subgroup of GGG that contains all elements that commute with all elements in GGG.

Z(G)={a∈G∣∀g∈G,ag=ga}Z(G)=\{a\in G\mid \forall g\in G, ag=ga\}Z(G)={a∈G∣∀g∈G,ag=ga}

this subgroup is normal and measure the “abelian” for a group.

Definition of the commutator of a group

Let GGG be a group and a,b∈Ga,b\in Ga,b∈G, the commutator [a,b][a,b][a,b] is defined as aba−1b−1aba^{-1}b^{-1}aba−1b−1.

[a,b]=e[a,b]=e[a,b]=e if and only if aaa and bbb commute.

Some additional properties:

  • [a,b]−1=[b,a][a,b]^{-1}=[b,a][a,b]−1=[b,a]

Definition of commutator subgroup

Let G′G'G′ be the subgroup of GGG generated by all commutators of GGG.

G′={[a1,b1][a2,b2]…[an,bn]∣a1,a2,…,an,b1,b2,…,bn∈G}G'=\{[a_1,b_1][a_2,b_2]\ldots[a_n,b_n]\mid a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n,b_1,b_2,\ldots,b_n\in G\}G′={[a1​,b1​][a2​,b2​]…[an​,bn​]∣a1​,a2​,…,an​,b1​,b2​,…,bn​∈G}

Then G′G'G′ is the subgroup of GGG.

  • Identity: [e,e]=e[e,e]=e[e,e]=e
  • Inverse: ([a1,b1],…,[an,bn])−1=[bn,an],…,[b1,a1]([a_1,b_1],\ldots,[a_n,b_n])^{-1}=[b_n,a_n],\ldots,[b_1,a_1]([a1​,b1​],…,[an​,bn​])−1=[bn​,an​],…,[b1​,a1​]

Some additional properties:

  • GGG is abelian if and only if G′={e}G'=\{e\}G′={e}
  • G′⊴GG'\trianglelefteq GG′⊴G
  • G/G′G/G'G/G′ is abelian
  • If NNN is a normal subgroup of GGG, and G/NG/NG/N is abelian, then G′≤NG'\leq NG′≤N.

Group acting on a set

Definition for group acting on a set

Let GGG be a group, XXX be a set, XXX is a GGG-set or GGG acts on XXX if there is a map

G×X→XG\times X\to XG×X→X (g,x)↦g⋅x ( or simply g(x))(g,x)\mapsto g\cdot x\, (\text{ or simply }g(x))(g,x)↦g⋅x( or simply g(x))

such that

  1. e⋅x=x,∀x∈Xe\cdot x=x,\forall x\in Xe⋅x=x,∀x∈X
  2. g2⋅(g1⋅x)=(g2g1)⋅xg_2\cdot(g_1\cdot x)=(g_2 g_1)\cdot xg2​⋅(g1​⋅x)=(g2​g1​)⋅x

Group action is a homomorphism

Let XXX be a GGG-set, g∈Gg\in Gg∈G, then the function

σg:X→X,x↦g⋅x\sigma_g:X\to X,x\mapsto g\cdot xσg​:X→X,x↦g⋅x

is a bijection, and the function ϕ:G→SX,g↦σg\phi:G\to S_X, g\mapsto \sigma_gϕ:G→SX​,g↦σg​ is a group homomorphism.

Definition of orbits

We define the equivalence relation on XXX

x∼y  ⟺  y=g⋅x for some gx\sim y\iff y=g\cdot x\text{ for some }gx∼y⟺y=g⋅x for some g

So we get a partition of XXX into equivalence classes: orbits

Gx≔{g⋅x∣g∈G}={y∈X∣x∼y}Gx\coloneqq \{g\cdot x|g\in G\}=\{y\in X|x\sim y\}Gx:={g⋅x∣g∈G}={y∈X∣x∼y}

is the orbit of XXX.

x,y∈Xx,y\in Xx,y∈X either Gx=GyGx=GyGx=Gy or Gx∩Gy=∅Gx\cap Gy=\emptysetGx∩Gy=∅.

X=⋃x∈XGxX=\bigcup_{x\in X}GxX=⋃x∈X​Gx.

Definition of isotropy subgroup

Let XXX be a GGG-set, the stabilizer (or isotropy subgroup) corresponding to x∈Xx\in Xx∈X is

Gx={g∈G∣g⋅x=x}G_x=\{g\in G|g\cdot x=x\}Gx​={g∈G∣g⋅x=x}

GxG_xGx​ is a subgroup of GGG. Gx≤GG_x\leq GGx​≤G.

  • e⋅x=xe\cdot x=xe⋅x=x, so e∈Gxe\in G_xe∈Gx​
  • If g1,g2∈Gxg_1,g_2\in G_xg1​,g2​∈Gx​, then (g1g2)⋅x=g1⋅(g2⋅x)=g1⋅x(g_1g_2)\cdot x=g_1\cdot(g_2\cdot x)=g_1 \cdot x(g1​g2​)⋅x=g1​⋅(g2​⋅x)=g1​⋅x, so g1g2∈Gxg_1g_2\in G_xg1​g2​∈Gx​
  • If g∈Gxg\in G_xg∈Gx​, then g−1⋅g=x=g−1⋅xg^{-1}\cdot g=x=g^{-1}\cdot xg−1⋅g=x=g−1⋅x, so g−1∈Gxg^{-1}\in G_xg−1∈Gx​

Orbit-stabilizer theorem

If XXX is a GGG-set and x∈Xx\in Xx∈X, then

∣Gx∣=(G:Gx)= number of left cosets of Gx=∣G∣∣Gx∣|Gx|=(G:G_x)=\text{ number of left cosets of }G_x=\frac{|G|}{|G_x|}∣Gx∣=(G:Gx​)= number of left cosets of Gx​=∣Gx​∣∣G∣​

Theorem for orbit with prime power groups

Suppose XXX is a GGG-set, and ∣G∣=pn|G|=p^n∣G∣=pn for some prime ppp. Let XGX_GXG​ be the set of all elements in XXX whose orbit has size 111. (Recall the orbit divides XXX into disjoint partitions.) Then ∣X∣≡∣XG∣mod  p|X|\equiv |X_G|\mod p∣X∣≡∣XG​∣modp.

Corollary: Cauchy’s theorem

If ppp is prime and p∣(∣G∣)p|(|G|)p∣(∣G∣), then GGG has a subgroup of order ppp.

This does not hold when ppp is not prime.

Consider A4A_4A4​ with order 121212, and A4A_4A4​ has no subgroup of order 666.

Corollary: Center of prime power group is non-trivial

If ∣G∣=pm|G|=p^m∣G∣=pm, then Z(G)Z(G)Z(G) is non-trivial. (Z(G)≠{e}Z(G)\neq \{e\}Z(G)={e})

Proposition: Prime square group is abelian

If ∣G∣=p2|G|=p^2∣G∣=p2, where ppp is a prime, then GGG is abelian.

Classification of small order

Let GGG be a group

  • ∣G∣=1|G|=1∣G∣=1
    • G={e}G=\{e\}G={e}
  • ∣G∣=2|G|=2∣G∣=2
    • G≃Z2G\simeq\mathbb{Z}_2G≃Z2​ (prime order)
  • ∣G∣=3|G|=3∣G∣=3
    • G≃Z3G\simeq\mathbb{Z}_3G≃Z3​ (prime order)
  • ∣G∣=4|G|=4∣G∣=4
    • G≃Z2×Z2G\simeq\mathbb{Z}_2\times \mathbb{Z}_2G≃Z2​×Z2​
    • G≃Z4G\simeq\mathbb{Z}_4G≃Z4​
  • ∣G∣=5|G|=5∣G∣=5
    • G≃Z5G\simeq\mathbb{Z}_5G≃Z5​ (prime order)
  • ∣G∣=6|G|=6∣G∣=6
    • G≃S3G\simeq S_3G≃S3​
    • G≃Z3×Z2≃Z6G\simeq\mathbb{Z}_3\times \mathbb{Z}_2\simeq \mathbb{Z}_6G≃Z3​×Z2​≃Z6​

Proof

∣G∣|G|∣G∣ has an element of order 222, namely bbb, and an element of order 333, namely aaa.

So e,a,a2,b,ba,ba2e,a,a^2,b,ba,ba^2e,a,a2,b,ba,ba2 are distinct.

Therefore, there are only two possibilities for value of ababab. (a,a2a,a^2a,a2 are inverse of each other, bbb is inverse of itself.)

If ab=baab=baab=ba, then GGG is abelian, then G≃Z2×Z3G\simeq \mathbb{Z}_2\times \mathbb{Z}_3G≃Z2​×Z3​.

If ab=ba2ab=ba^2ab=ba2, then G≃S3G\simeq S_3G≃S3​.

  • ∣G∣=7|G|=7∣G∣=7
    • G≃Z7G\simeq\mathbb{Z}_7G≃Z7​ (prime order)
  • ∣G∣=8|G|=8∣G∣=8
    • G≃Z2×Z2×Z2G\simeq\mathbb{Z}_2\times \mathbb{Z}_2\times \mathbb{Z}_2G≃Z2​×Z2​×Z2​
    • G≃Z4×Z2G\simeq\mathbb{Z}_4\times \mathbb{Z}_2G≃Z4​×Z2​
    • G≃Z8G\simeq\mathbb{Z}_8G≃Z8​
    • G≃D4G\simeq D_4G≃D4​
    • G≃G\simeqG≃ quaternion group {e,i,j,k,−1,−i,−j,−k}\{e,i,j,k,-1,-i,-j,-k\}{e,i,j,k,−1,−i,−j,−k} where i2=j2=k2=−1i^2=j^2=k^2=-1i2=j2=k2=−1, (−1)2=1(-1)^2=1(−1)2=1. ij=lij=lij=l, jk=ijk=ijk=i, ki=jki=jki=j, ji=−kji=-kji=−k, kj=−ikj=-ikj=−i, ik=−jik=-jik=−j.
  • ∣G∣=9|G|=9∣G∣=9
    • G≃Z3×Z3G\simeq\mathbb{Z}_3\times \mathbb{Z}_3G≃Z3​×Z3​
    • G≃Z9G\simeq\mathbb{Z}_9G≃Z9​ (apply the corollary, 9=329=3^29=32, these are all the possible cases)
  • ∣G∣=10|G|=10∣G∣=10
    • G≃Z5×Z2≃Z10G\simeq\mathbb{Z}_5\times \mathbb{Z}_2\simeq \mathbb{Z}_{10}G≃Z5​×Z2​≃Z10​
    • G≃D5G\simeq D_5G≃D5​
  • ∣G∣=11|G|=11∣G∣=11
    • G≃Z11G\simeq\mathbb{Z}_11G≃Z1​1 (prime order)
  • ∣G∣=12|G|=12∣G∣=12
    • G≃Z3×Z4G\simeq\mathbb{Z}_3\times \mathbb{Z}_4G≃Z3​×Z4​
    • G≃Z2×Z2×Z3G\simeq\mathbb{Z}_2\times \mathbb{Z}_2\times \mathbb{Z}_3G≃Z2​×Z2​×Z3​
    • A4A_4A4​
    • D6≃S3×Z2D_6\simeq S_3\times \mathbb{Z}_2D6​≃S3​×Z2​
    • ??? One more
  • ∣G∣=13|G|=13∣G∣=13
    • G≃Z13G\simeq\mathbb{Z}_{13}G≃Z13​ (prime order)
  • ∣G∣=14|G|=14∣G∣=14
    • G≃Z2×Z7G\simeq\mathbb{Z}_2\times \mathbb{Z}_7G≃Z2​×Z7​
    • G≃D7G\simeq D_7G≃D7​

Lemma for group of order 2p2p2p where ppp is prime

If ppp is prime, p≠2p\neq 2p=2, and ∣G∣=2p|G|=2p∣G∣=2p, then GGG is either abelian ≃Z2×Zp\simeq \mathbb{Z}_2\times \mathbb{Z}_p≃Z2​×Zp​ or G≃DpG\simeq D_pG≃Dp​

Ring

Definition of ring

A ring is a set RRR with binary operation +++ and ⋅\cdot⋅ such that:

  • (R,+)(R,+)(R,+) is an abelian group.
  • Multiplication is associative: (a⋅b)⋅c=a⋅(b⋅c)(a\cdot b)\cdot c=a\cdot (b\cdot c)(a⋅b)⋅c=a⋅(b⋅c).
  • Distribution property: a⋅(b+c)=a⋅b+a⋅ca\cdot (b+c)=a\cdot b+a\cdot ca⋅(b+c)=a⋅b+a⋅c, (b+c)⋅a=b⋅a+c⋅a(b+c)\cdot a=b\cdot a+c\cdot a(b+c)⋅a=b⋅a+c⋅a. (Note that ⋅\cdot⋅ may not be abelian, may not even be a group, therefore we need to distribute on both sides.)
Note

a⋅b=aba\cdot b=aba⋅b=ab will be used for the rest of the sections.

Properties of rings

Let 000 denote the identity of addition of RRR. −a-a−a denote the additive inverse of aaa.

  • 0⋅a=a⋅0=00\cdot a=a\cdot 0=00⋅a=a⋅0=0
  • (−a)b=a(−b)=−(ab)(-a)b=a(-b)=-(ab)(−a)b=a(−b)=−(ab), ∀a,b∈R\forall a,b\in R∀a,b∈R
  • (−a)(−b)=ab(-a)(-b)=ab(−a)(−b)=ab, ∀a,b∈R\forall a,b\in R∀a,b∈R

Definition of commutative ring

A ring (R,+,⋅)(R,+,\cdot)(R,+,⋅) is commutative if a⋅b=b⋅aa\cdot b=b\cdot aa⋅b=b⋅a, ∀a,b∈R\forall a,b\in R∀a,b∈R.

Definition of unity element

A ring RRR has unity element if there is an element 1∈R1\in R1∈R such that a⋅1=1⋅a=aa\cdot 1=1\cdot a=aa⋅1=1⋅a=a, ∀a∈R\forall a\in R∀a∈R.

Definition of unit

Suppose RRR is a ring with unity element. An element a∈Ra\in Ra∈R is called a unit if there is b∈Rb\in Rb∈R such that a⋅b=b⋅a=1a\cdot b=b\cdot a=1a⋅b=b⋅a=1.

In this case bbb is called the inverse of aaa.

Definition of division ring

If every a≠0a\neq 0a=0 in RRR has a multiplicative inverse (is a unit), then RRR is called a division ring.

Definition of field

A commutative division ring is called a field.

Units in Zn\mathbb{Z}_nZn​ is coprime to nnn

More generally, [m]∈Zn[m]\in \mathbb{Z}_n[m]∈Zn​ is a unit if and only if gcd⁡(m,n)=1\operatorname{gcd}(m,n)=1gcd(m,n)=1.

Integral Domains

Definition of zero divisors

If a,b∈Ra,b\in Ra,b∈R with a,b≠0a,b\neq 0a,b=0 and ab=0ab=0ab=0, then a,ba,ba,b are called zero divisors.

Zero divisors in Zn\mathbb{Z}_nZn​

[m]∈Zn[m]\in \mathbb{Z}_n[m]∈Zn​ is a zero divisor if and only if gcd⁡(m,n)>1\operatorname{gcd}(m,n)>1gcd(m,n)>1 (mmm is not a unit).

Corollaries of integral domain

If RRR is a integral domain, then we have cancellation property ab=ac,a≠0  ⟹  b=cab=ac,a\neq 0\implies b=cab=ac,a=0⟹b=c.

Units with multiplication forms a group

If RRR is a ring with unity, then the units in RRR forms a group under multiplication.

Fermat’s and Euler’s Theorems

Fermat’s little theorem

If ppp is not a divisor of mmm, then mp−1≡1mod  pm^{p-1}\equiv 1\mod pmp−1≡1modp.

Corollary of Fermat’s little theorem

If m∈Zm\in \mathbb{Z}m∈Z, then mp≡mmod  pm^p\equiv m\mod pmp≡mmodp.

Euler’s totient function

Consider Z6\mathbb{Z}_6Z6​, by definition for the group of units, Z6∗={1,5}\mathbb{Z}_6^*=\{1,5\}Z6∗​={1,5}.

ϕ(n)=∣Zn∗∣=∣{1≤x≤n:gcd(x,n)=1}∣\phi(n)=|\mathbb{Z}_n^*|=|\{1\leq x\leq n:gcd(x,n)=1\}|ϕ(n)=∣Zn∗​∣=∣{1≤x≤n:gcd(x,n)=1}∣

Euler’s Theorem

If m∈Zm\in \mathbb{Z}m∈Z, and gcd(m,n)=1gcd(m,n)=1gcd(m,n)=1, then mϕ(n)≡1mod  nm^{\phi(n)}\equiv 1\mod nmϕ(n)≡1modn.

Theorem for existence of solution of modular equations

ax≡bmod  nax\equiv b\mod nax≡bmodn has a solution if and only if d=gcd⁡(a,n)∣bd=\operatorname{gcd}(a,n)|bd=gcd(a,n)∣b And if there is a solution, then there are exactly ddd solutions in Zn\mathbb{Z}_nZn​.

Ring homomorphisms

Definition of ring homomorphism

Let R,SR,SR,S be two rings, f:R→Sf:R\to Sf:R→S is a ring homomorphism if ∀a,b∈R\forall a,b\in R∀a,b∈R,

  • f(a+b)=f(a)+f(b)  ⟹  f(0)=0,f(−a)=−f(a)f(a+b)=f(a)+f(b)\implies f(0)=0, f(-a)=-f(a)f(a+b)=f(a)+f(b)⟹f(0)=0,f(−a)=−f(a)
  • f(ab)=f(a)f(b)f(ab)=f(a)f(b)f(ab)=f(a)f(b)

Definition of ring isomorphism

If fff is a ring homomorphism and a bijection, then fff is called a ring isomorphism.
